title: ExoMol molecular line lists -- XLII: Rovibronic molecular line list for the low-lying states of NO

author_details.name: Qianwei Qu
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: Sergei N. Yurchenko
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: and Jonathan Tennyson
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

journal: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
abstract: An accurate line list, called XABC, is computed for nitric oxide which covers its pure rotational, vibrational and rovibronic spectra. A mixture of empirical and theoretical electronic transition dipole moments are used for the final calculation of rovibronic -- , -- and -- which correspond to the , and band systems, respectively, as well as minor improvements to transitions within the ground state. The work is a major update of the ExoMol NOname line list. It provides a high-accuracy NO ultraviolet line list covering the complicated regions where the - states interact. XABC provides comprehensive data for the lowest four doublet states of NO in the region of () for the analysis of atmospheric NO on Earth, Venus or Mars, other astronomical observations and applications. The data is available via www.exomol.com.
body_text:
-
 

 

 

Introduction

 Nitric oxide (NO) is widely distributed in the universe.
 The molecule was observed in the atmosphere
 of Venus and Mars , where it is one of the emission sources of the UV nightglow .
 Gerin et al. detected transitions of NO in the dark clouds
 L134N and TMC1 .
 reported their analysis of transitions of N2O to NO
 in the core region of the Sagittarius B2
 and evaluated the N/O chemical network.
 The first detection of extragalactic NO by 
 helps us to understand the chemistry of galaxy NGC 253.
 NO has yet to be detected in the atmosphere of an exoplanet but is thought likely to be important in the atmospheres of rocky exoplanets .
 The detection of NO in the astronomical objects relies on knowledge of the corresponding spectral lines of the molecule so accurate NO line list plays significant role in the processes.
 
 There are several available
 high-resolution line lists of the NO ground state.
 The HITRAN database by is widely used for
 investigations of the Earth's atmosphere and other room temperature studies.
 For higher temperature applications, the NO line list in the HITEMP database has been 
 recently updated based upon use of the ExoMol line list .
 The CDMS database by contains long-wavelength data including lines with
 hyperfine-structure;
 data on two vibrational bands of are available on the CDMS website. 
 published the lines list
 as a part of the ExoMol database .
 , available for six isotopologues,
 contains 21688 states and 2409810 transitions for 14N16O. 

 The line lists discussed above provide comprehensive coverage of ground state transitions but do not allow
 for the transitions involving different electronic states.
 LIFBASE by is an exception.
 It contains line lists for the
 ( - ), ( - ), 
 ( - ) and ( - ) NO band systems
 and provides relative cross sections via an interactive front-end program.
 However, there are some issues with the LIFBASE database, which appears to be no longer maintained, such as 
 some bands of high intensities (e.g. and )
 are missing.
 Due to the strong interactions and curve crossings between its electronically excited state,
 it is not easy to model the excited states of NO.
 Similar interactions 
 were also reported in the works discussing other
 nitrogen oxide e.g. NO3
 .
 To address this problem,
 we proposed a method based on
 directly diagonalizing a rovibronic matrix using the variational nuclear motion program . 
 Our model used a diabatic representation to resolve 
 the energy structures of - coupled states.
 A (measured active rotation vibration energy levels) analysis 
 was used to produce a set of empirical energy levels of spectroscopic accuracy.
 These levels were then used by Duo to produce curves and couplings that give an accurate
 description of the , and states for levels up to 63000 above the ground state; 
 the previous \ line list already provides
 a good spectroscopic model for the state.
 This new spectroscopic model provides an excellent starting point 
 for computing a line list for
 the , and band systems of NO. 

 This work aims to provide
 a line list which is both accurate and complete covering 
 the rovibrational transitions within the state
 and the rovibronic transitions belonging to the 
 other three important band systems,
 i.e.,
 , 
 and 
 as shown in Fig.\,. 
 Rovibronic energy levels and wavefunctions
 were taken from our published model . The TDMC of 
 - was taken from the literature , while the - and - TDMCs, involving two strongly coupled electronic states and , 
 are constructed in the present work.
To make the line list more accurate, some calculated values are replaced with empirical ones and the associated uncertainties were provided. 
These issues are discussed in turn in the following sections
 
 
 

Transition dipole moments

 Our previous study explored the difficulty of preforming
 \ calculations of NO in some detail; 
 fundamentally the problem arises because the and states
 are effectively Rydberg-like in character which means their curves follow that of the tightly bound NO+ ion
 , 
 while the state is a valence state with a much flatter curve which crosses the others, see Fig.\, (a).
 Discontinuities arise in the potential energy curves (PECs) and other curves
 due to the state (avoided) crossings and interactions.
 The quality of transition dipole moment curves (TDMC)
 is strongly affected by the complicated behaviour of the excited state wavefunctions which were computed at the
 complete active space self-consistent field (CASSCF) and multi-reference configuration interaction (MRCI) levels using MOLPRO .
 We therefore
 adopted a pragmatic approach to determining the TDMCs in which 
 the TDMCs were modified through a comparison with experimental data.

 
 
 
 
 

Range of calculation
 
 The calculation setup is
 consistent with those of
 and
 our previous work :
 the internuclear distance, , varied from
 0.6 4 , 
 which in was discretised by 701 uniformly spaced grid points as part of the sinc DVR 
 (discrete variable representation) basis set.
 In the final calculations, 60, 15, 30, and 10 contracted vibrational basis functions were retained 
 for the , , and states, respectively.
 
 
 As we do not include interactions with higher electronic states in our model, the highest vibrational levels of the , and states were
 limited to 8, 18, and 4, respectively.
 The vibrational wavefunctions of these levels 
 are shown in Fig.\, (a),
 where they are vertically shifted to their vibrational energies.
 The global energy limit was chose to be 65000 .
 

 

 state
 

For the - transitions,
we used the empirical TDMC constructed by as a fourth-order polynomial (see the green solid curve shown in Fig.\, (b).
 We chose not to use an TDMC of - 
 although the one depicted by the green dash curve 
 in Fig.\, (b) looks very similar to the empirical one.
 We found that use of different active spaces in MOLPRO
 gave TDMCs that were very different in both shape and amplitude; this behaviour is 
 discussed by .
 We thus had to compare these curves with the empirical TDMC and
 select a similar one; this
 procedure is neither ` ' nor `empirical'.
 The empirical TDMC function of was based on 
 the lower vibrational levels of the state. As a result,
 their polynomial diverges at distances .
 We chose to use this TDMC unaltered as the vibrational wavefunctions 
 decay rapidly to zero in this region so our line list 
 is insensitive to the behaviour of the TDMC at these values of .

 
 updated the TDMC polynomial coefficients of using radiative lifetimes of NO ( ) which they measured using time-resolved laser-induced fluorescence. Their transition dipole moment is larger in magnitude than that of Luque and Crosley. We use the TDMC of Luque and Crosley as a balance between measured radiative lifetimes and the intensities ( integrated cross section ) of the measured by 
; see Fig.\,.

 
 

 - coupled states
 
 

The adiabatic PECs and have the same symmetry and 
therefore form an avoided crossing as shown by our \ PECs computed using MOLPRO,
see circles in Fig. (a). 
In order to avoid discontinuities in the various curves, 
including the TDMCs, 
here we follow our diabatic model with the PECs shown in Fig.\, (a).
Off-diagonal matrix elements were introduced to 
represent the electronic state interaction as follows:
 
where and 
are two diabatic potentials and is
a bell-shaped coupling curve. The effect of this representation is illustrated in in Fig.\,, 
although the above matrix elements are introduced into the rovibronic Hamiltonian matrix rather than directly diagonalizing Eq. to generate adiabatic potentials shown in this figure. 

 

Due to the (adiabatic) avoided crossing between and , 
the adiabatic TDMCs - and - exhibit erratic behaviour 
in the interaction region with their values rising and dropping sharply near 1.18 , 
as shown by the blue and red circles in Fig.\, (b). 
The interaction center (about 1.18 ) is close to the 
equilibrium bond length (about 1.15 ) of the state. 
Thus, even a slight change in the TDMC in this region along 
internuclear distance axis can dramatically change the calculated transition intensities.
 
 
In the diabatic model, the - and - TDMC are smooth curves (see Fig. (b)) which do not show erratic behaviour in the interaction region and therefore are no longer the most sensitive factor 
in intensity calculations. The coupling between states in this model is controlled by and relies on the quality of the rovibronic and wavefunctions, which can be accurately determined by our technique of fitting theoretical curves using experimental energies. 
 
The original PECs for and and the corresponding TDMC of - were computed using a high level of theory, CASSCF\&MRCI+Q/cc-pVQZ with the 
active space, where indicates the occupied and closed orbitals in the irreducible representations , , and of the C2v point group (shown as red and blue circles in Fig.\,). These adiabatic data were then diabatized 
to produce curves shown in Fig.\, as dotted curves. In order to improve the quality of the intensity calculations, the diabtaic TDMCs were then further empirically adjusted as follows. 
The - TDMC was scaled using a combination of the measured lifetimes of and integrated cross sections of . 
The scaling factor of 1.17 was chosen as a balance between these two experiments.
The scaled - TDMC is shown as a blue solid line in Fig\,. 
Although the - TDMC diverges from its original trend for the internuclear distances shorter than 1 , this does not affect our calculation 
as the corresponding vibrational wavefunctions nearly vanish there. 
 
The TDMC of - required special care; 
we first fitted a quadratic polynomial to the adiabatic
 values, 
shown as the red dash curve in Fig.\,, and then scaled it based on the absorption intensities measured by .
Since the TDMC of - influences the intensities of higher bands (i.e. ), it was important to obtain a global agreement for all intensities, including the system. This is illustrated in Fig.\,, where the calculated spectrum of NO in the region of 
200 is compared to the experimental intensities by . 
 
All curves comprising our spectroscopic mode, including the TDMCs, are provided in the supplementary material to this paper as a input file. 
 
 

Line list calculation

A rovibronic line list for NO was constructed using the program and the spectroscopic model described above. For the detailed description of the calculation see our
previous work .

 The line list consists of 4596666 transitions 
 between 30811 states of the four low-lying 
 electronic states , , and , (21668, 
 1209, 6873 and 1041, respectively), 
 covering with rovibronic wavenumber cutoffs 
 of 53000 ( , same as in ) and 63000 (all other states). 
 These energy cutoffs are smaller than the basis set limit 
 to avoid any truncation problems near 63000 . 
 In line with the ExoMol data structure , the line list is represented by two files, a .states (states) file
 and a .trans (transitions) file. 
 Table\, gives an extract of the .states file. 
The .trans (transitions) file contains the Einstein- coefficients 
calculated with the spectroscopic model of this work
and constitutes our new line list. 
Table\, gives an extract from the .trans file. 

 
 The current spectroscopic model uses improved parameters for the state compared to . As a consequence, the new model shows better agreement with the effective Hamiltonian SPFIT/SPCAT energies of NO also presented in , see Fig.\,. 
 Due to the change in the model and consequently the wavefunctions, 
 the Einstein- coefficients between the states of 
 as well as the corresponding lifetimes have also changed. 
 The energies of the lower rovibronic states ( and ) of 
 were replaced with the ones which were
 calculated by using the programs SPFIT and SPCAT ,
 based on the work of .
 These states are labeled with EH to indicate that 
 they were calculated from effective Hamiltonian, which
 replaces the label e (i.e. empirical)
 used in for these EH levels.
 The energies of the other states of 
 were shifted from the results of , 
 using the same strategy as , 
 to avoid energy jumps
 above or .
 These shifted states were labeled with Sh
 while they were labeled with c (i.e. calculated) in .

 We also replaced the energies of the , and states 
 with energies where available,
 these states are labeled Ma in the .states file. 
 The jumps between and energies 
 in the excited electronic states are negligible. 
 Therefore, we did not shift the other calculated energies 
 of the , or states
 and labeled these states with Ca.

 For this line list we also introduced an extra column 
 containing the energy uncertainties 
 (i.e. Column 5, ) for each state. 
 The uncertainties of , and were
 taken from MARVEL analysis where available.
 Otherwise, they were constructed 
 according to corresponding vibrational and rotational quantum numbers 
 using the algorithm shown in Fig.\,. 
 The estimation of uncertainties of the state is a bit more complicated but is based on the same idea, see Fig.\,.

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 

landscape 
 
landscape

The partition function was computed using the standard summation 
over energies using levels from our final line list.
Figure\, compares the Total Internal Partition Sums (TIPS) partition functions, , 
of and this work. 
As can be seen from the red curve, 
i.e., ,
the partition function difference is very small because the thermodynamic properties are generally not very sensitive to small variations of higher-lying energies as in the state, while the , and energies (not considered in TIPS) are too high to make 
obvious difference for the temperature below 3500 K considered here. 
 

 

 
 
 
 
With the assumption of local thermal equilibrium, we calculated absorption spectra of NO using the new line list . An overview of the absorption spectrum fo different temperatures below 63000 or longward 1600 
is shown in Fig.\,.
 
 

 
 

Comparisons
 
 
 Unless otherwise indicated, 
 the following calculations were executed with ,
 which is a program 
 for generating lifetimes, spectra, partition function etc.,
 from molecular line lists.

Lifetimes
 
 
Lifetimes for individual states of , , and are plotted against the energies in Fig.\,. The vibronic lifetimes of ( to ) and ( to ) are compared with experimental values (where available) in Tables and , respectively. The calculated lifetimes of state agree well with those measured by . As we used their TDMC, the agreement means that gave similar vibrational wavefunctions as the RKR (Rydberg– Klein–Rees) ones they used. Our computed lifetimes for the state are larger than those of previous works.
 
 
The funnel-like shapes of the dependence of lifetimes on the energy shown in Fig.\, (b) are caused by the interactions between the and vibronic levels. The lifetimes decrease to much smaller values when rotational quantum numbers are trapped in these funnels. Apart from the electronic state interaction, the observed lifetimes are further shortened by predissociation. 
The dot-dash lines in Fig.\, illustrate the first dissociation limit of NO. As the current version of does not allow for predissociation, the calculated lifetimes to the right of the dot-dash lines are expected to be larger than the observed ones. For example, the calculated lifetimes of 
 are of order 10 ns whereas the measured ones can be as short as several nanoseconds .
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Absorption spectra

 
Figure\, compares the experimental absorption intensities of measured by and theoretical intensities calculated with . With the TDMC of , our calculations gives higher intensities than the observed ones for the transitions of and branches. Thus, if we had used the TDMC of , would further amplify the intensities of all branches, worsening agreement with observation.

 
 
 
The \,-\, interaction have small effect on the intensities of
lower seven bands. Figure\, compares the experimental intensities of measured by and theoretical intensities calculated with . 
The relative cross section values calculated by LIFBASE are compared with values in Fig.\,. The values of LIFBASE are scaled according to the peak of band.
 
 
 
 
 
The band is the strongest one at 295 K in the \,- interaction region and the intensities of the transitions in this band are plotted in Fig.\,. The figure demonstrates the overall agreement between experimental and theoretical values but also exposes defects in our model showing 
the interaction model for \,- 
is not perfect.

 
 
Note that, the spectra in Figs. , and were calculated using the pure energies before they been replaced by MARVEL or EH values. However the difference between the experimental and calculated lines is indistinguishable at this scale.

Conclusions

 
 Here we present a new line list for \ called which covers transitions between the ground electronic state and the four lowest-lying states, , , and . The line list combines effective Hamiltonian (SPFIT), and calculated ( ) energies, providing high-accuracy line positions. Combined with our - , - and - transition dipole moments, the diabatic model predicts the transition intensities which agree well with the measured values. The line list is part of ExoMol project and available from (www.exomol.com www.exomol.com )
 and CDS database
 (http://cdsarc.u-strasbg.fr cdsarc.u-strasbg.fr ).

 Qianwei Qu acknowledges the financial support from University College London

 This work was supported by the STFC Projects No. ST/M001334/1 and ST/R000476/1, and ERC Advanced Investigator Project 883830.
 The authors acknowledge the use of the UCL Myriad, Grace and Kathleen High Performance Computing Facilities and associated support services in the completion of this work.

Data Availability

 
The model file and calculated partition function file are
attached as a supplementary materials.
The states and transition files of can be downloaded from
www.exomol.com www.exomol.com and
http://cdsarc.u-strasbg.fr cdsarc.u-strasbg.fr . The open access programs \ and \ are available from https://github.com/exomol github.com/exomol .
